---
title: How can you responsibly use AI?
image: '/public/cover-images/DALLE_LEGO-scientist-discovering-metadata.jpg'
description: "AI is in common use in research and education, how can you use it safely?"
categories: ["Discover & Initiate"]
---

# Safety & Privacy

## Privacy concerns when using AI

The same privacy concerns you have when doing your research normally also hold when working with AI (or LLMs). Any data you are working with should be anonymised or pseudonymised to protect the respondents. Any proprietary information or data is not to be shared beyond those already with access. Any data related to national defence cannot leave the strict group it has been shared with.

To help you consider whether using an AI is safe, the following question can be useful:
    -   Is this data personal?
    -   Is this data sensitive?
    -   Is this data confidential?
    -   Are there intellectual property implications for uploading data?
    -   Do I have consent or a legal basis to process this data?

Or, more directly.
    -   Would you hand this text or data directly to Sam Altman?
    -   Would you post this data on Instagram?
    -   Would you leave a printed copy of the data on a park bench?

If the answer was yes to (any of) the first 4 or no to (any of) the last 3 then you should not be using any service hosted or stored outside of your own computer.

***Decision tree would do well here***

## How can I use an AI safely in my work?

Do not use any AI tools that are not officially approved by VU IT.
Do not upload data that you would not happily give to a company for unknown use
Avoid uploading data without proper safeguards
Examine the data retention policy of the service. How long will they keep this for? Will it be shared with 3rd parties?
Look at Nebula and consider if it is suitable for your use case
Consider running a model locally (on your own computer)
Check that the output it provides is accurate
Avoid outsourcing too much to AI
Keep in mind that all models have inbuilt bias (just like us!)
Don’t use a model to make decisions that put lives or jobs at risk


****NOTES****

Safety and Privacy
Uploading sensitive research data
Sharing personal data without proper safeguards
Risk of data retention or unintended reuse
Data classification 
Is this data personal?
Is this data sensitive?
Is this data confidential?
Are there intellectual property implications for uploading data?
Do I have consent or a legal basis to process this data?
Maybe add information about connecting with Privacy Champion in these points.

#***** Should ethics be a separate article?

Ethics (built-in bias)
AI may generate incorrect or fabricated information
Embedded bias in training data
Lack of transparency in model decision-making
Potential harm to human subjects in the case of experiments using AI
Connection can be formed with the model, anthropomorphism
Data shared with models is now accessible to others
Water use
Legality of training data
Electricity use
Respondents might not want their data to be analysed by AI
Data leaving the EU

Privacy and Security Considerations
Do NOT Input
Personal identifiable information
Student records
Health data
Confidential research data
Unpublished manuscripts
Defence industry-related data
Legal documents
Anything you would not personally hand to Sam Altman
Proprietary institutional data
*Unless you or your institution has a formal agreement and approved AI tools (Nebula)
	

GDPR Considerations
Lawfulness, Fairness, and Transparency
Inform individuals how their data is used
Have a legal basis for processing
Avoid hidden automated decision-making

Purpose Limitation
Data must only be used for its stated purpose.

Data Minimization
Only process what is strictly necessary
Accuracy
Ensure AI outputs affecting individuals are verified.

Storage Limitation
Do not store personal data longer than necessary.

Integrity and Confidentiality
Ensure appropriate security measures.

Automated Decision-Making (Article 22 GDPR)
Individuals have rights regarding:
Automated decisions
Profiling
AI-driven grading or admissions decisions.
Human oversight is essential. 

Key GDPR Concepts in Relation to AI Usage (Dewitte, 2025)
GDPR applies when personal data is processed for AI training or output.
Controllers and processors must be identified, and responsibilities allocated, even in complex AI ecosystems.
Advances in AI can make supposedly “anonymised” data re-identifiable, thus widening GDPR’s scope.
AI systems often reuse data collected for one purpose to train models for another; controllers must show such processing is both lawful and necessary.
The GDPR demands transparent communication to data subjects (e.g. why their data is being processed, by whom, and for what purpose); AI’s complexity creates challenges for explainability in this case. 
AI systems qualify as “high-risk” processing, making DPIA necessary and providing risk mitigation.
In summary, researchers need to ensure that AI systems respect fundamental data protection rights.


Best Practices For Responsible AI Usage
Transparency
Disclose AI usage/assistance in research or coursework.
Human Oversight
AI is meant to assist and NOT replace human expertise and/or judgement. 
Documentation
Record how AI tools were used in research methodology.
Risk Assessment
Conduct an impact assessment for high-risk AI uses
Secure Use 
Use institutionally approved platforms
Enable two-factor authentication
Avoid public/free tools for sensitive data?
AI Citation?
“Portions of this manuscript were drafted with the assistance of an AI Language Model and subsequently reviewed and edited by the author.”
